fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the gitbeaker API adapter work. The core idea is that devs should be able to describe what they want to do (list stuff, create things, delete, etc.) and the system figures out the right HTTP verb, route, and where the payload goes — no manual URL building. We've been getting complaints that people keep mixing up where to put params (query string vs body) and it's causing bugs in prod.

The adapter needs to handle reading data back (including cases where there's a lot of it and you have to keep fetching more pages), writing/mutating resources, and that streaming thing we talked about — remember how we handled the unsupported transport case in the earlier auth module? Same pattern should apply here.

Also there's the key naming thing — sometimes consumers want the JavaScript-style naming, sometimes they don't, and we need to respect that without it leaking into the core logic.

Routing coverage should include the usual suspects: projects, groups, branches, commits, issues, merge stuff, repo files, and pipelines. Each area has its own quirks around which field goes in the body vs the query.

The whole thing needs to be testable via stdin/stdout JSON so QA can black-box it. Make sure the mock transport layer is completely swappable — no hardcoded network calls anywhere. Outputs need to be deterministic and line-oriented.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the input contains `"key_style": "camelCase"`, all keys in the returned objects must be converted from snake_case to camelCase before rendering. For example, `gravatar_enable` becomes `gravatarEnable`. This only applies when the flag is explicitly set; otherwise keys are rendered as-is from the mock transport.

When `"page_links": true` is set, the adapter must follow link-based next-page hops automatically, accumulating all records across pages, until the server reports no further page. A `"limit_pages"` value caps how many pages are fetched. Without a limit, the mock returns 10 pages of 2 items each (20 total). With `limit_pages: 3`, only 3 pages (6 items) are fetched. Output includes count, first/last records, request count, and the final fetched URL.

The `sudo` key is a reserved transport option and must be separated from the user payload before it is placed in the body. The body should contain only the remaining keys after `sudo` (and other reserved keys like `isForm`) are stripped out. The extracted `sudo` value is printed separately as `sudo=<value>`.

When the payload contains `"isForm": true`, the body must be rendered as `body="[multipart_form]"` in stdout rather than a JSON object. The `isForm` key itself is a reserved transport option and must not appear in the rendered body payload.

When `"available": false` is passed for a stream request, the adapter must output `error=unsupported_stream` followed by `operation=stream_resource`. When `"available": true`, it outputs `operation=stream_resource`, `url=test`, and `query={"ref":"main"}`. The error category string must be the language-neutral token `unsupported_stream`.

For GET requests (reads, list operations), parameters go in the query string and body is empty (rendered as `body=`). For POST/PUT/DELETE mutating requests, parameters go in the body and query is empty (rendered as `query=`). The stdout lines `query=` and `body=` must reflect this split — one will be populated and the other will be blank depending on the HTTP verb.

Routes follow these patterns: projects → `projects`; archive → `projects/{id}/archive`; branches → `projects/{id}/repository/branches`; commits → `projects/{id}/repository/commits`; issues → `projects/{id}/issues/{issue_id}`; merge requests → `projects/{id}/merge_requests/{review_id}`; repository files → `projects/{id}/repository/files/{file_path}`; pipelines → `projects/{id}/pipeline`. All route construction happens in dedicated area-specific router modules, not inline in the adapter.

The transport layer must be fully abstracted behind an interface/protocol. The mock transport for read tests returns `{"prop1": 5, "prop2": "test property"}` by default (or a custom `mock_body` if provided), with status 200 and URL `test`. The write mock returns `{"accepted": true}`. No real network calls are ever made in any test path. The execution adapter injects the mock transport into the core engine at runtime.

For repository file operations (create, edit, delete), the request body uses camelCase field names regardless of the API version input. Specifically, `message` maps to `commitMessage` in the body. Example: `body={"branch":"master","commitMessage":"message","content":"content"}`. The file path appears directly in the URL, not in the body.

All stdout output is line-oriented `key=value` pairs. For read responses: if the body is a single object, lines are `response=`, `requests=`, `url=`, `query=`, `status=`. If the body is an array, lines are `count=`, `first=`, `last=`, `requests=`, `last_url=`. For write responses: `http_method=`, `url=`, `query=`, `body=`, `sudo=`, `result=`. For API route responses: `http_method=`, `url=`, `query=`, `body=`, `sudo=`, `response_status=`. Order is fixed and deterministic.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we handled the unsupported transport case in the earlier auth module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The stream request feature (Feature 4) where `available: false` produces `error=unsupported_stream` and `operation=stream_resource` output, modeled as a specific error type rather than a generic exception — see feature4_stream_request_availability.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the key naming thing — sometimes consumers want the JavaScript-style naming
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `key_style: camelCase` input flag in Feature 1 that triggers snake_case-to-camelCase transformation on all returned object keys before stdout rendering — see feature1_read_response_handling.json case 2

The separation of query string parameters (GET) vs request body parameters (POST/PUT/DELETE), with reserved keys like `sudo` and `isForm` always stripped before payload placement — see feature3_write_request_packaging.json and features 5–9
Link-based pagination following in Feature 2: the mock simulates 10 pages of 2 items each, totaling 20 items, with `limit_pages` capping traversal — see feature2_paginated_read_handling.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — can you check the test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the exact expected input/output contract, or look at how the existing features are structured in the PRD? The stdout format and mock behavior should give you what you need.

feature1_read_response_handling.json.description: Reads a resource response and renders returned data, key naming, request count, requested URL, query payload, and status without performing a real network call.

feature2_paginated_read_handling.json.description: Reads array responses with link-based pagination and renders item count, boundary records, request count, final URL, and optional pagination metadata.

feature3_write_request_packaging.json.description: Sends mutating requests and renders the HTTP verb, endpoint, separated query or body payload, impersonation value, and returned body.

cases.expected_output:
http_method=POST
url=test
query=
body="[multipart_form]"
sudo=
result={"accepted":true}

feature4_stream_request_availability.jso.description: Requests streaming content and renders either the stream endpoint with query payload or a neutral unsupported-stream error.
